groups.name: Fictional Towns Canonically Built Over a Supernatural Portal or Convergence
groups.explanation: Sunnydale (Buffy) sits on a Hellmouth; Hawkins, Indiana (Stranger Things) sits above the Upside Down gate; Mystic Falls (Vampire Diaries) is built on a supernatural ley-line convergence; Castle Rock (Castle Rock on Hulu) sits atop a dimensional rift. All four towns are explicitly explained in-show as existing over a supernatural gateway.

groups.name: Shows Where the Entire Fictional Company Collapsed or Was Sold in the Series Finale
groups.explanation: Dunder Mifflin (The Office) was absorbed by Sabre then later sold off; Sabre (The Office) collapsed due to a product recall scandal mid-series; Waystar Royco (Succession) was sold to GoJo in the final episode against the family's wishes; Veep's Selina Meyer's political operation dissolved completely in the finale. Each tile represents a fictional institution that definitively ceased to exist in its show's final episode.

groups.name: Actors Who Won Their First Emmy for Playing a Character With a Diagnosed Mental Illness
groups.explanation: Claire Danes won her first Emmy for Carrie Mathison (bipolar disorder, Homeland); N/A won his first Emmy for Fleabag's Priest (grief/depression arc); N/A won his first Emmy for John Luther (obsessive compulsive tendencies, diagnosed in-show); N/A won his BAFTA/Emmy recognition for Malcolm Tucker whose rage disorder is clinically referenced. Players will assume these are simply 'acclaimed British drama actors' — but the real link is the specific Emmy/major award for a mentally ill character.

groups.name: TV Characters Whose Death Was Revealed to Have Happened Before the Scene We're Watching
groups.explanation: N/A (tile_1) — the Priest's departure is framed as a death of connection revealed retroactively through Fleabag's direct address; Nate Fisher (tile_2, Six Feet Under) dies and scenes after his death reframe earlier moments as already posthumous; John Luther (tile_3) — in Luther: The Fallen Sun, scenes are revealed to precede his apparent death; N/A (tile_4, Homeland) in the finale is revealed to be operating from a perspective where her old life is already gone/dead. These characters all experience a narrative reveal where the audience realizes the character's 'present' scenes were already post-death or post-loss when we watched them.
